question: Explain the functional boundaries of NuScale.

nuscale_ch01_0117.title: Figure 1.7-3f: Piping and In strumentation Diagram Legends
nuscale_ch01_0117.summary: This section provides the legend for piping and instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs) used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. It lists and describes various symbols representing sample probes, pressure and temperature sensors, flow measurement devices, and other instrumentation and accessory devices, ensuring standardized interpretation of the diagrams.

nuscale_ch01_0118.title: 1.8 Interfaces with Certified Design
nuscale_ch01_0118.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities. The section also notes that COL applicants must provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant certified design and the site-specific design provided in the Combined License (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities of the COL applicant. The section also notes that COL applicants must provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0395.title: DSRS 9.5.2, Rev 0: Communication SystemsII.4 Design, Fabrication, Erection, Construction, Testing, and Inspection of SSC to Meet 10 CFR 50.55a
nuscale_ch01_0395.summary: Section II.4 of DSRS 9.5.2, Rev 0, states that requirements for the design, fabrication, erection, construction, testing, and inspection of structures, systems, and components (SSC) to meet 10 CFR 50.55a are not applicable to the NuScale standard plant design. No provisions or responsibilities are assigned under this criterion in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report.

nuscale_ch01_0630.title: SRP 14.3, (March 2007): Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance CriteriaII.1 Acceptability of the Scope of ITAAC
nuscale_ch01_0630.summary: This section reviews the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report’s conformance with regulatory criteria related to Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC). It notes that NuScale partially or fully conforms to relevant sections of the Standard Review Plan (SRP) and Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS), with some criteria specifically applicable to either Design Certification (DC) or Combined License (COL) applicants. Certain SRP sections are deemed not applicable, as they pertain to other licensing actions such as power uprates.

nuscale_ch01_0160.title: DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0: Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical EquipmentII.25 Design and Procurement Specifications
nuscale_ch01_0160.summary: This section outlines NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria related to the environmental qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment, as well as the design and procurement specifications. It notes that certain requirements, such as DSRS 3.11 II.25, are not applicable to NuScale and are instead items for the COL (Combined License) applicant. The section also confirms NuScale’s compliance with several ASME Code and SRP requirements for piping systems and threaded fasteners, while clarifying that some guidance applies only to BWR plants and is therefore not relevant.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes key plant features of special interest in the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), which house essential but nonsafety-related systems and backup generators. It also highlights human factors considerations, such as fail-safe design, automation, digital controls, and workload management, aimed at minimizing human error and meeting regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0727.title: DSRS 15.2.8, Rev 0: Feedwater System Pipe Break Inside and Outside Containment (PWR)II.3 Calculated Site Boundary Doses
nuscale_ch01_0727.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for feedwater system pipe breaks inside and outside containment, specifically regarding calculated site boundary doses. It states that the analysis conforms to the applicable requirements, with no exceptions noted for this criterion.

nuscale_ch01_0438.title: SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3: Condensate Cleanup SystemII.1 Maintain direct cycle BWR plant water quality to avoid corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14)
nuscale_ch01_0438.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria for systems related to condensate cleanup and associated components. It notes that certain requirements, such as those for direct cycle BWRs, are not applicable to NuScale’s design, while for indirect cycle PWR water quality, NuScale conforms but with different secondary chemistry requirements due to its unique steam generator configuration. The document also highlights where exemptions or alternative criteria are applied.

nuscale_ch01_0439.title: SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3: Condensate Cleanup SystemII.2 Maintain indirect cycle PWR water quality to avoid corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14)
nuscale_ch01_0439.summary: The section outlines NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for maintaining indirect cycle PWR water quality to prevent corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, as required by GDC 14. It states that the NuScale steam generator design differs from traditional designs, with primary water outside and secondary water inside the tubes, and no steam generator blowdown, resulting in unique secondary chemistry requirements.

edges.description: Section A provides diagram legends for instrumentation and accessory devices used in NuScale systems, while Section B defines the interface requirements and boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific elements, connecting them through the identification and demarcation of functional system boundaries.

Section A provides detailed symbols and legends for instrumentation and accessory devices used in NuScale's systems, while Section B outlines the interface requirements and identifies the boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific elements, connecting them through the need to define what components and devices fall within the certified design's functional boundaries.

Section A and Section B are essentially identical, both addressing the interface requirements and boundaries between the NuScale certified design and the site-specific design in the COL application, referencing the same figures and tables.

Section A outlines regulatory requirements and responsibilities for communication systems in NuScale's design, while Section B provides diagram legends that help identify the physical boundaries and components of such systems, thus Section B specifies the detailed elements referenced in Section A's general requirements.

Section A discusses the responsibilities and provisions for communication systems within the NuScale design, specifying that certain emergency response communication systems are the responsibility of the COL applicant, while Section B defines the interface requirements and boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific (COL) design, thus both sections are connected by delineating which functional elements and responsibilities fall within the certified design versus the site-specific scope.

Section A identifies that certain communication systems for emergency response are the responsibility of the COL applicant, while Section B defines the interface requirements and boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific (COL) design, thus clarifying which systems fall within NuScale's functional boundaries.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria and testing requirements for NuScale's systems, while Section B provides detailed diagram legends that define the symbols and components referenced in those system descriptions, connecting the regulatory scope to the physical system boundaries.

Section A discusses the acceptability and scope of inspections, tests, analyses, and acceptance criteria (ITAAC) for the NuScale design, while Section B defines the interface requirements and physical boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific elements, thus Section B provides the specific boundary information that Section A's ITAAC scope must respect.

Section A discusses the regulatory criteria and testing requirements (ITAAC) for the NuScale design, while Section B specifies the interface requirements and delineates the boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific elements, thus Section B provides the concrete boundary information that Section A's regulatory framework applies to.

Section A specifies that certain emergency communication system responsibilities are assigned to the COL applicant, while Section B outlines that the scope and acceptability of Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC) for these systems are also determined at the COL stage, connecting both sections through the allocation of functional responsibilities and verification processes for NuScale's boundaries.

Section A references regulatory requirements and conformance criteria for equipment and piping systems, while Section B provides the detailed diagram legends that specify the actual components and boundaries referenced in those requirements, thus Section B provides the concrete details necessary to interpret and apply the general regulatory framework described in Section A.

Section A references regulatory criteria and conformance items that are addressed in Section B's discussion of the interface requirements and boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific design, connecting the regulatory specifications to the delineation of functional boundaries.

Section A references regulatory requirements and conformance items that are deferred to the COL applicant, while Section B specifies the interface requirements and delineates the boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific (COL) design, thus Section B provides the concrete functional boundaries that Section A's regulatory references pertain to.

Section A describes the functional areas and systems housed within the Central Utility Building, while Section B provides the diagram legends for instrumentation and piping components that may be present in or relevant to those systems, thus Section B specifies the symbols and details used to represent the types of equipment and boundaries described in Section A.

Section A describes specific plant features and structures, while Section B defines the interface requirements and explicitly references Section 1.2 (from Section A) as identifying the boundaries of the certified design, thus connecting the detailed features to their regulatory boundary context.

Section A describes specific plant features and structures, some of which are identified in Section B as being within the certified design boundaries, thereby providing concrete examples of what is included within the functional boundaries discussed in Section B.

Section A references regulatory conformance and analysis results for NuScale system boundaries, while Section B provides the diagram legends necessary to interpret the detailed piping and instrumentation drawings that define those functional boundaries.

Section B defines the interface and general boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific elements, which provides the context for interpreting the system-specific regulatory conformance analyses in Section A.

Section A discusses how NuScale's safety analysis report conforms to regulatory criteria for specific system events, while Section B defines the interface requirements and physical boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific elements, thus Section B provides the structural context necessary to interpret the functional boundaries referenced in Section A.

Section A outlines regulatory conformance and qualification requirements for mechanical and electrical equipment, while Section B describes the physical plant features and systems (such as the Central Utility Building) that must comply with these requirements, connecting the regulatory boundaries to the actual plant systems.

Section A specifies the design and procurement requirements for mechanical and electrical equipment, which define the functional boundaries that Section B relies on when analyzing the consequences of feedwater system pipe breaks and associated safety system responses.

Section A describes the physical and functional components of the NuScale plant, while Section B references regulatory criteria and safety analysis results that these plant features must support or satisfy, connecting the plant's functional boundaries to compliance with safety requirements.

Section A discusses the regulatory requirements and conformance for NuScale's systems, while Section B provides the diagram legends necessary to interpret the detailed piping and instrumentation diagrams referenced in Section A, thus enabling understanding of system boundaries.

Section A details the regulatory conformance and system boundaries for specific NuScale systems, while Section B defines the interface and physical boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific elements, with both sections connecting through their delineation of what is included within the NuScale functional and regulatory scope.

Section A details how the NuScale design conforms to specific regulatory criteria for system boundaries and functions, while Section B identifies and illustrates the interface boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific elements, thus both sections together define and clarify the functional boundaries of NuScale by connecting regulatory requirements to physical and procedural demarcations.

Section A outlines the regulatory functions and boundaries of NuScale's systems, while Section B provides the diagram legends necessary to interpret the detailed piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually define those system boundaries.

Section A details the regulatory criteria and system functions that define what is included in the NuScale certified design, while Section B explicitly identifies and illustrates the boundaries and interfaces between the certified design (including those systems) and site-specific elements, thus Section B specifies the functional boundaries referenced in Section A.

Section A details the regulatory criteria and system functions that define what is included in the NuScale certified design, while Section B specifies the interface requirements and explicitly identifies the boundaries between the certified design and site-specific design, thus Section B provides the structural delineation that contextualizes the functional scope described in Section A.

Section A and Section B both address the requirement to maintain water quality to avoid corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14), but Section A refers to direct cycle BWRs while Section B refers to indirect cycle PWRs, highlighting how the functional boundary of NuScale is defined by its applicability to PWR criteria.
